front: Composer: 'father of ethnomusicology'; used an Edison machine with Zoltán Kodály to collect folk recordings in Eastern Europe and Turkey
Composer: 'night music' style — spooky ambient dissonances in slow movements evoking natural sounds; arch form (ABCBA)
Composer: died of leukemia; left unfinished viola concerto for William Primrose and final 17 bars of Piano Concerto No. 3, both completed by Tibor Serly
Composer: 'piano year' of 1926 produced Piano Sonata, Out of Doors, Nine Little Piano Pieces, and Piano Concerto No. 1 — all percussive in character
Composer: wrote Contrasts clarinet trio for Benny Goodman; first movement is a verbunkos (Hungarian recruiting dance)
Composer: Romanian Folk Dances — six pieces based on tunes collected near the Hungarian-Transylvanian border; begins with 'Stick Dance' and 'Sash Dance'
Concerto: 'Game of Pairs' second movement opens with solo side drum, then pairs of instruments in parallel intervals (bassoons in sixths); commissioned by Koussevitzky for the Boston Symphony
Concerto: 'Intermezzo interrotto' fourth movement parodies Shostakovich's Leningrad Symphony invasion theme and quotes Lehár's The Merry Widow
Concerto: 'Intermezzo interrotto' bass trombone B–F glissando is unplayable on modern B-flat bass trombones (originally written for an F bass trombone)
String Quartet: snap pizzicato ('Bartók pizzicato') — strings snapped against fingerboard, most prominent in String Quartet No. 4 finale
String Quartet: all four movements of the sixth (final) quartet begin with a slow Mesto introduction; arch form used in quartets 4 and 5
String Quartet: fifth quartet scherzo is 'alla bulgarese' in (4+2+3)/8 time, borrowing Bulgarian aksak rhythms
String Quartet: first quartet's opening Lento fugue is a 'funeral dirge' referencing Bartók's unrequited love for violinist Stefi Geyer
Opera: only Bartók opera; two roles (Bluebeard and Judith); seven doors conceal a torture chamber, lake of tears, and three previous wives; minor second is the 'blood motif'
Ballet: thieves force a girl to lure men; the mandarin refuses to die from stabbing and hanging until the girl accepts his love; trombone glissandi descending a minor third represent his desire
Suite: 153 piano pieces in order of increasing difficulty; dedicated to son Péter; Volume VI ends with 'Six Dances in Bulgarian Rhythm'; inspired Crumb's Makrokosmos
Concerto: No. 1 opens with low B-naturals pounded by soloist and timpani, then menacing brass fanfare; No. 2 built in arch form with Adagio interrupted by Presto
Suite: two string groups on opposite sides of the stage; third movement invokes 'night music' with xylophone and timpani glissandi
back: Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta (Bartók)
work: Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta
